topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an irritating persistence. Liam slumped in his chair, the textbook feeling heavier than usual, and his jaw was tight. He was trying to focus on macroeconomic theory, but his attention kept snagging on the notification icon on his phone. It was a picture of Professor Albright, smiling broadly, captioned with a quote about intellectual curiosity. Liam let out a low, involuntary sigh, pushing his phone face-down under his notes. He really wished he could just tune everything out. Every time he looked up, the small, insistent buzz of his device seemed to amplify the general air of tediousness that clung to the study hall. He picked up his pen, but his hand trembled slightly, the energy for concentration utterly depleted.

[story 2]

Maya slammed her laptop shut with a sharp snap that made a few nearby students jump. She stared at the screen, her brow furrowed deep enough to create permanent creases. Professor Davies had posted a video clip from a recent lecture, and beneath it, a comment from him, tagging her handle. Maya felt a flush creep up her neck, hot and unwelcome. She deliberately scrolled through her feed, avoiding the little pop-up that reminded her of the interaction. She wanted nothing more than to be invisible, to disappear into the beige walls of the classroom. The sheer audacity of it made her fists clench at her sides, a tight, restless energy vibrating just beneath her skin.

[story 3]

Ethan dragged his feet across the linoleum floor of the hallway, his backpack straps digging uncomfortably into his shoulders. He was late for his next class, and the early morning rush was making him feel heavy and slow. As he rounded the corner toward his locker, he saw it—a post from his history teacher, a casual snapshot from a weekend trip. Ethan stopped dead. A peculiar tightness seized his chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. He stared at the screen, his expression hardening into a mask of utter disinterest, trying desperately to force his mind to process the absurdity of the situation without reacting. He just wanted the hallway to end.
